label: Argentina / Spanish
ladderNote: Current Rioplatense usage checked against 2025–2026 Argentine media, social posts, and local forums; aging or weakly local labels omitted.

levels.term: No hay nada / no pasa nada
levels.meaning: Nothing going on.
There's chemistry / a vibe. Used casually by Argentines.
Flirting; showing interest. Common in BA speech (e.g. local Reddit).
We're getting to know each other.
We made out (kissed with tongue). Local Argentine slang.
One-night hookup. Current, direct Argentine phrasing; the older touch and go remains understood but is increasingly generational.
Regular sexual hookup without a relationship label; current and crude.
Casual sex/dating partner without formal commitment. The feminine is chonga; the verb is chonguear.
Friends with benefits; more friendship history than a chongo.
We're seeing each other; still soft on labels.
We're dating toward possible noviazgo; current and more securely Argentine than saliente.
Situationship / almost-relationship. Argentine media and everyday speech.
Exclusive without calling each other novios. Newer phrasing; shows up in Argentine social media.
Official boyfriend/girlfriend; individually, novio or novia.
My partner / we're a couple; more adult than novio.
We said “I love you” to each other. Te amo is stronger than the broader, everyday te quiero.
Living together as a couple (everyday colloquial).
Engaged.
Legal civil cohabitation registration (Código Civil) — formal/legal, not casual slang.
Mother/father of my child—the usual Argentine Spanish description for the other parent, often an ex-partner.
Married.
Married with children (also a famous Argentine sitcom title).
